description: Native app surfaces phase A4 (docs/design/native-app-surfaces.md): iOS app contexts run end-to-end on a managed simulator via the lazily-installed XCUITest driver. startSurface resolves/boots the newest installed iPhone simulator through simctl, find/click/screenshot act on it through the XCUITest semantic mapping, and closeSurface terminates the app; the run-end sweep shuts down only simulators Doc Detective booted. Gated to iOS targets via runOn; on Windows/Linux every test lands SKIPPED with actionable macOS/Xcode/simctl guidance. The first XCUITest session on a cold runner builds WebDriverAgent via xcodebuild (minutes), so startSurface timeouts are generous. Built-in stock apps (Settings, Calendar) are used so no app payload is fetched. The named-device create-boot path is covered by unit tests (test/ios-simulator.test.js) rather than here: creating a fresh simulator forces a second, highly variable cold WDA build (10-25 min) that doesn't fit the CI budget, and the effectful `simctl create` is a thin wrapper over the thoroughly unit-tested planner.

tests.description: The mobile-browser support matrix gates unsupported iOS pairs: a chrome+ios context SKIPs deterministically on every host (safari is the supported iOS browser, phase A5). Supported mobile-web runs (safari on the managed simulator) are exercised in the mobile-web-ios group, not here.

Launch Settings by bundle ID on the default (newest installed iPhone) simulator, wait for a stable row to exist, find it through the XCUITest column, screenshot the app, then close it. The real end-to-end PASS on a capable macOS host; SKIPs elsewhere.

Two stock apps (Settings + Calendar) share one simulator session; acting on each surface foregrounds it via activateApp, proving multi-app-per-simulator switching. Each surface is screenshotted (which forces the foreground switch) and then closed.
